The free (homogeneous) oscillation gives a **pure tone** whose frequency is intrinsic to the system. That intrinsic behaviour is exactly what the **complementary function** of a homogeneous ODE describes. Once the forcing (your hand) is removed, the system continues to "remember" its own natural response. ### 1.4 Salient Properties (Linear Algebra Connection) * **Linearity** ensures the **Superposition Principle**: if $y_1$ and $y_2$ are two solutions, then $c_1 y_1 + c_2 y_2$ is *also* a solution for **arbitrary constants** $c_1, c_2 \in \mathbb{R}$. * The **General Solution** of a second-order homogeneous linear ODE always contains exactly **two arbitrary constants** (matching the order of the ODE). * The pair $\{y_1, y_2\}$ must be **linearly independent** for the general solution to be complete; otherwise, we lose one arbitrary constant and hence one degree of freedom. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Structure of the General Solution For $y'' + P(x)\,y' + Q(x)\,y = 0$ on an interval $I$ where $P, Q$ are continuous, the **General Solution** is: $$ \boxed{\,y(x) = c_1\, y_1(x) + c_2\, y_2(x)\,} $$ provided $y_1$ and $y_2$ are two **linearly independent** solutions. Two solutions are linearly independent if neither is a constant multiple of the other. ### 2.2 The Wronskian — Detecting Linear Independence For two solutions $y_1$ and $y_2$, the **Wronskian** $W(y_1, y_2)$ is the determinant: $$ \boxed{\,W(y_1, y_2) = \begin{vmatrix} y_1 & y_2 \\ y_1' & y_2' \end{vmatrix} = y_1 y_2' - y_1' y_2\,} $$ > [!NOTE] **Abel's Identity (KTU Favourite — frequently asked for 3 marks)** > For the ODE $y'' + P(x)\,y' + Q(x)\,y = 0$, the Wronskian of any two solutions satisfies: > $$\boxed{\,W(y_1, y_2)(x) = C\,\exp\!\left(-\int P(x)\,dx\right)\,}$$ > where $C$ is a constant determined by initial conditions. > * If $W \neq 0$ on $I$ → solutions are **linearly independent**. > * If $W = 0$ for all $x \in I$ → solutions are **linearly dependent**. ### 2.3 Existence and Uniqueness Theorem Given $y'' + P(x)\,y' + Q(x)\,y = 0$ with $P, Q$ continuous on an open interval $I$ containing $x_0$, and the initial conditions $y(x_0) = y_0$, $y'(x_0) = y_0'$, there exists a **unique solution** on $I$. > [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Utility** > This theorem guarantees that a physical system (RLC circuit, spring-mass-damper, vibrating beam) described by a second-order linear ODE is **completely determined** by its **state at one instant** — position and velocity. This is the mathematical foundation of *state-space modelling* in control engineering. ### 2.4 Three Principal Solution Methods (KTU 2024 Module 2 Syllabus) | # | Method | Applicable To | Auxiliary Tool | |---|---|---|---| | **M1** | **Characteristic / Auxiliary Equation** | Constant coefficients: $P, Q$ are constants | Solve a quadratic in $m$ | | **M2** | **Reduction of Order** | One solution $y_1$ is known; second is unknown | $y_2 = y_1 \int \dfrac{e^{-\int P\,dx}}{y_1^{2}}\,dx$ | | **M3** | **Cauchy–Euler (Equidimensional)** | Coefficients are powers of $x$: $a x^2 y'' + b x y' + c y = 0$ | Substitution $x = e^{t}$ | ### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Formula Box — Memorize This Completely** | Formula | Expression | Condition / When to Use | |---|---|---| | Standard form | $y'' + P(x)y' + Q(x)y = 0$ | Always — first step of every problem | | Characteristic equation | $m^{2} + P\,m + Q = 0$ | Only when $P, Q$ are **constants** | | Case 1: Real distinct roots $m_1 \neq m_2$ | $y = c_1 e^{m_1 x} + c_2 e^{m_2 x}$ | Discriminant $\Delta > 0$ | | Case 2: Real equal roots $m_1 = m_2 = m$ | $y = (c_1 + c_2 x)\,e^{m x}$ | Discriminant $\Delta = 0$ | | Case 3: Complex roots $m = \alpha \pm i\beta$ | $y = e^{\alpha x}\left[c_1 \cos(\beta x) + c_2 \sin(\beta x)\right]$ | Discriminant $\Delta < 0$ | | Abel's identity | $W(x) = C \exp\!\left(-\int P\,dx\right)$ | For any two solutions | | Reduction of order | $y_2 = y_1 \int \dfrac{\exp(-\int P\,dx)}{y_1^{2}}\,dx$ | When one solution is known | | Cauchy–Euler transformation | $x = e^{t}\quad \Rightarrow \quad x \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{dy}{dt},\;\; x^{2}\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} - \dfrac{dy}{dt}$ | For $a x^{2} y'' + b x y' + c y = 0$ | | Cauchy–Euler auxiliary eq. | $a\,m(m-1) + b\,m + c = 0$ | Same as characteristic but for Euler form | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Significance * **Electrical:** RLC circuit (free response, no source) → $L\dfrac{d^{2}q}{dt^{2}} + R\dfrac{dq}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{C}q = 0$ — underdamped, critically damped, overdamped cases correspond exactly to **complex, equal, real-distinct** root cases. * **Mechanical:** Undamped / damped vibration analysis. * **Control Systems:** Stability of a 2nd-order plant depends entirely on the sign of the real part of roots (Lyapunov stability). * **Signal Processing:** Natural modes of a second-order filter. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] **This section is exhaustive — every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped.** ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Solution of Constant-Coefficient Equation via Characteristic Equation **Statement:** Solve $y'' + p\,y' + q\,y = 0$ where $p, q$ are real constants. **Step 1: Assume an exponential trial solution.** Since differentiation of $e^{mx}$ gives a constant multiple of $e^{mx}$, it is the natural candidate. $$ \text{Let } y = e^{m x}, \quad y' = m\,e^{m x}, \quad y'' = m^{2} e^{m x} $$ **Step 2: Substitute into the ODE.** $$ \begin{aligned} m^{2} e^{m x} + p \cdot m\,e^{m x} + q \cdot e^{m x} &= 0 \\[4pt] e^{m x}\left(m^{2} + p\,m + q\right) &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Factor out the non-zero term.** Since $e^{mx} \neq 0$ for all real $x$, we must have: $$ \boxed{\,m^{2} + p\,m + q = 0\,} $$ This is the **Auxiliary (Characteristic) Equation**. **Step 4: Solve the quadratic** using $m = \dfrac{-p \pm \sqrt{p^{2} - 4q}}{2}$. Three cases arise based on the discriminant $D = p^{2} - 4q$: --- #### **Case A: $D > 0$ → Two Real Distinct Roots $m_1, m_2$** Both $e^{m_1 x}$ and $e^{m_2 x}$ are valid solutions, and they are linearly independent. Hence: $$ \boxed{\,y(x) = c_1\, e^{m_1 x} + c_2\, e^{m_2 x}\,} $$ *Verification (Linearity):* $y_1 = e^{m_1 x},\; y_2 = e^{m_2 x}$. $$ W = \begin{vmatrix} e^{m_1 x} & e^{m_2 x} \\ m_1 e^{m_1 x} & m_2 e^{m_2 x} \end{vmatrix} = (m_2 - m_1)\,e^{(m_1+m_2)x} \neq 0 $$ Since $m_1 \neq m_2$, $W \neq 0$, confirming independence. ✔ --- #### **Case B: $D = 0$ → Two Real Equal Roots $m_1 = m_2 = m$** Only **one** exponential solution $e^{mx}$ is obtained from the trial. We need a second, linearly independent solution. Try $y_2 = v(x)\,e^{mx}$ (variation of parameters in elementary form). $$ \begin{aligned} y_2 &= v\,e^{m x} \\ y_2' &= v'\,e^{mx} + v\,m\,e^{mx} \\ y_2'' &= v''\,e^{mx} + 2 v' m\,e^{mx} + v\,m^{2} e^{mx} \end{aligned} $$ Substitute into the ODE: $$ \begin{aligned} e^{mx}\!\left[v'' + 2 m v' + v m^{2}\right] + p\,e^{mx}\!\left[v' + v m\right] + q\,v\,e^{mx} &= 0 \\ e^{mx}\!\left[v'' + (2m + p)v' + (m^{2} + pm + q)v\right] &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ Since $m$ is a root, $m^{2} + pm + q = 0$. The equation reduces to: $$ v'' + (2m + p)v' = 0 $$ Set $w = v'$ to lower the order: $$ w' + (2m + p)w = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad w = A\,e^{-(2m+p)x} $$ But $m = -p/2$ (from $D = 0$), so $2m + p = 0$, hence $w = A$ (constant). Therefore $v' = A$, so $v = A x + B$. Choosing the part not proportional to $y_1 = e^{mx}$ (we already have $B e^{mx}$), we set $A = 1, B = 0$ giving $v = x$, hence $y_2 = x\,e^{mx}$. $$ \boxed{\,y(x) = (c_1 + c_2 x)\,e^{m x}\,} $$ *Verification of independence:* $$ W = \begin{vmatrix} e^{mx} & x e^{mx} \\ m e^{mx} & e^{mx}(1 + mx) \end{vmatrix} = e^{2mx}\left[1 + mx - mx\right] = e^{2mx} \neq 0 \;\;\checkmark $$ --- #### **Case C: $D < 0$ → Complex Conjugate Roots $m = \alpha \pm i\beta$** Here $\alpha = -p/2$ and $\beta = \sqrt{4q - p^{2}}/2$. The "solutions" are $e^{(\alpha + i\beta)x}$ and $e^{(\alpha - i\beta)x}$, which are **complex-valued**. Using Euler's formula $e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta$: $$ \begin{aligned} e^{(\alpha + i\beta)x} &= e^{\alpha x}\!\left[\cos(\beta x) + i\sin(\beta x)\right] \\ e^{(\alpha - i\beta)x} &= e^{\alpha x}\!\left[\cos(\beta x) - i\sin(\beta x)\right] \end{aligned} $$ Taking the **real** and **imaginary parts** as two real solutions (justified by linearity over $\mathbb{R}$): $$ y_1 = e^{\alpha x}\cos(\beta x), \qquad y_2 = e^{\alpha x}\sin(\beta x) $$ Their general linear combination gives: $$ \boxed{\,y(x) = e^{\alpha x}\!\left[c_1 \cos(\beta x) + c_2 \sin(\beta x)\right]\,} $$ *Verification:* $W = e^{2\alpha x}\!\left[\cos(\beta x)\cdot\beta\cos(\beta x) - (-\beta\sin(\beta x))\sin(\beta x)\right] = \beta e^{2\alpha x} \neq 0$ since $\beta \neq 0$. ✔ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reduction of Order (General Formula) **Problem:** Given one non-zero solution $y_1$ of $y'' + P(x)y' + Q(x)y = 0$, find a second linearly independent solution $y_2$. **Assumption:** Let $y_2 = v(x)\,y_1(x)$ where $v$ is an unknown function. **Step 1: Compute derivatives.** $$ \begin{aligned} y_2 &= v\, y_1 \\ y_2' &= v' y_1 + v\, y_1' \\ y_2'' &= v'' y_1 + 2 v' y_1' + v\, y_1'' \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Substitute into the ODE.** $$ v'' y_1 + 2 v' y_1' + v\, y_1'' + P(x)(v' y_1 + v\, y_1') + Q(x)\,v\,y_1 = 0 $$ **Step 3: Group terms by $v''$, $v'$, $v$.** $$ v'' y_1 + v'(2 y_1' + P y_1) + v\underbrace{(y_1'' + P y_1' + Q y_1)}_{=\,0\;\text{(since } y_1 \text{ is a solution)}} = 0 $$ The $v$ term vanishes because $y_1$ satisfies the ODE. We are left with: $$ v'' y_1 + v'(2 y_1' + P y_1) = 0 $$ **Step 4: Substitute $w = v'$** to reduce order: $$ w' y_1 + w(2 y_1' + P y_1) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{w'}{w} = -\frac{2 y_1' + P y_1}{y_1} = -2 \frac{y_1'}{y_1} - P(x) $$ **Step 5: Integrate.** $$ \ln \vert w \vert = -2 \ln \vert y_1 \vert - \int P(x)\,dx $$ $$ w = v' = \frac{1}{y_1^{2}}\,e^{-\int P(x)\,dx} $$ **Step 6: Integrate once more** to obtain $v$, then $y_2 = v\,y_1$: $$ \boxed{\,y_2(x) = y_1(x) \int \frac{\exp\!\left(-\int P(x)\,dx\right)}{y_1^{2}(x)}\,dx\,} $$ This is the **Reduction of Order Formula** — a KTU staple. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cauchy–Euler Equation Transformation **Problem:** Solve $a x^{2} y'' + b x y' + c y = 0$ for $x > 0$, where $a, b, c$ are constants, $a \neq 0$. **Step 1: Substitute $x = e^{t}$**, so $t = \ln x$ and $\dfrac{dt}{dx} = \dfrac{1}{x}$. **Step 2: Apply the chain rule.** $$ \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{dy}{dt}\cdot \frac{dt}{dx} = \frac{1}{x}\frac{dy}{dt} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{dy}{dt} $$ **Step 3: Differentiate again.** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} &= \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{1}{x}\frac{dy}{dt}\right) = -\frac{1}{x^{2}}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{1}{x}\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{dy}{dt}\right) \\ &= -\frac{1}{x^{2}}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{1}{x}\cdot\frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}}\cdot\frac{dt}{dx} = -\frac{1}{x^{2}}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{1}{x^{2}}\frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} \end{aligned} $$ Multiplying by $x^{2}$: $$ x^{2}\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = \frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} - \frac{dy}{dt} $$ **Step 4: Substitute into the original equation.** $$ a\!\left(\frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} - \frac{dy}{dt}\right) + b\!\left(\frac{dy}{dt}\right) + c\,y = 0 $$ $$ a\,\frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} + (b - a)\,\frac{dy}{dt} + c\,y = 0 $$ This is a **constant-coefficient** ODE in $t$! Try $y = e^{mt}$: $$ a m^{2} + (b - a) m + c = 0 $$ Or equivalently, written directly in $x$-language (the "Cauchy–Euler auxiliary equation"): $$ \boxed{\,a\,m(m-1) + b\,m + c = 0\,} $$ **Step 5: Solve for $m$** and revert using $t = \ln x$: | Roots of $a m^{2} + (b-a)m + c = 0$ | Solution in $x$ | |---|---| | Real distinct $m_1 \neq m_2$ | $y = c_1 x^{m_1} + c_2 x^{m_2}$ | | Real equal $m_1 = m_2 = m$ | $y = (c_1 + c_2 \ln x)\,x^{m}$ | | Complex $\alpha \pm i\beta$ | $y = x^{\alpha}\!\left[c_1 \cos(\beta \ln x) + c_2 \sin(\beta \ln x)\right]$ | > [!NOTE] **Note on the Repeated-Root Case for Euler Equation** > When the auxiliary equation has a double root $m$, the second solution is $x^{m}\ln x$, **not** $x \cdot x^{m}$. This contrasts with the constant-coefficient case where the extra factor is $x$, not $\ln x$. --- ### 3.4 Worked Example — Mixed Case (Full Analytical Walkthrough) **Solve:** $y'' - 3y' + 2y = 0,\quad y(0) = 2,\quad y'(0) = 3$. **Step 1: Characteristic equation.** $$ m^{2} - 3m + 2 = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad (m-1)(m-2) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad m_1 = 1,\; m_2 = 2 $$ Real distinct roots → Case A. **Step 2: General solution.** $$ y(x) = c_1 e^{x} + c_2 e^{2x} $$ **Step 3: Apply initial conditions.** $$ y'(x) = c_1 e^{x} + 2 c_2 e^{2x} $$ From $y(0) = 2$: $$ c_1 + c_2 = 2 \tag{I} $$ From $y'(0) = 3$: $$ c_1 + 2 c_2 = 3 \tag{II} $$ **Step 4: Solve the linear system.** Subtract (I) from (II): $c_2 = 1$. Substitute back: $c_1 = 1$. **Step 5: Particular solution.** $$ \boxed{\,y(x) = e^{x} + e^{2x}\,} $$ *Verification:* $y(0) = 1 + 1 = 2$ ✔; $y'(0) = 1 + 2 = 3$ ✔. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Classification & Solution Flowchart The following Mermaid diagram captures the **decision logic** a student must follow when encountering a second-order homogeneous linear ODE in the KTU exam. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: 2nd Order Homogeneous Linear ODE"] --> B{"Are coefficients
constant?"} B -- "YES" --> C["Write Characteristic
Equation m^2 + pm + q = 0"] C --> D{"Discriminant
D = p^2 - 4q"} D -- "D greater than 0
Real Distinct" --> E["y = c1 e to m1x
plus c2 e to m2x"] D -- "D equal to 0
Real Equal" --> F["y = c1 plus c2 x
times e to mx"] D -- "D less than 0
Complex alpha plus i beta" --> G["y = e to alpha x times
c1 cos beta x plus c2 sin beta x"] B -- "NO" --> H{"Is it a Cauchy Euler
ax2 y double prime plus
bxy prime plus cy = 0?"} H -- "YES" --> I["Substitute x = e to t"] I --> J["Get constant coeff ODE in t
Solve for m"] J --> K["Revert: t = ln x
Apply 3 cases as above"] H -- "NO" --> L{"Is one solution
y1 already known?"} L -- "YES" --> M["Use Reduction of Order
y2 = y1 integral of
e to neg integral of P dx
divided by y1 squared dx"] L -- "NO" --> N["Advanced methods
not in KTU Module 2
Series or Variation of Parameters"] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Physical Interpretation The block diagram below maps the **mathematical structure** of a second-order homogeneous linear ODE to its **physical realization** in an RLC electrical circuit (free response, source disconnected). ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PHYS["Physical System: Source-Free RLC Circuit"] R["Resistor R
Dissipation element"] L["Inductor L
Inertia element"] C["Capacitor C
Storage element"] SW["Switch open
No external forcing
RHS = 0"] end subgraph MATH["Mathematical Model"] EQ["L q double prime plus R q prime
plus q over C = 0"] CH["Characteristic equation
Ls squared plus Rs plus 1 over C = 0"] SOL["q of t = A e to s1 t plus B e to s2 t
or overdamped or underdamped"] end PHYS --> MATH EQ --> CH CH --> SOL SOL -.->|"Initial conditions
q of 0 and i of 0"| COEFFS["Solve for A and B
Final closed form"] ``` ### 4.3 Concept Map — Wronskian and Linear Independence ```mermaid flowchart TD W1["Two solutions y1 and y2
of y double prime + Py prime + Qy = 0"] --> W2["Compute Wronskian
W = y1 y2 prime minus y1 prime y2"] W2 --> W3{"W equals 0
for all x in I?"} W3 -- "YES" --> W4["Linearly Dependent
Cannot form general solution"] W3 -- "NO" --> W5["Linearly Independent
General solution:
y = c1 y1 + c2 y2"] W6["Abel Identity
W = C exp minus integral of P dx"] -.->|"Use to compute W
without explicit y1, y2"| W2 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Set B]** > Define a second-order homogeneous linear differential equation. Give one example and state the order and degree. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **second-order homogeneous linear differential equation** is an equation of the form $$ y'' + P(x)\,y' + Q(x)\,y = 0 $$ where $P(x)$ and $Q(x)$ are functions of $x$ only (or constants), and the right-hand side is identically zero. It is *second-order* because the highest derivative is $y''$, and *homogeneous* because there is no term independent of $y$ and its derivatives. **Example:** $y'' + 4y' + 3y = 0$. * **Order = 2**, **Degree = 1**. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP Set A]** > State **Abel's Identity** for the Wronskian of two solutions of a second-order linear homogeneous ODE. **Model Answer (3 marks):** If $y_1$ and $y_2$ are any two solutions of $y'' + P(x)y' + Q(x)y = 0$ on an interval $I$ where $P$ and $Q$ are continuous, then their Wronskian is given by: $$ \boxed{\,W(y_1, y_2)(x) = C \cdot e^{-\int P(x)\,dx}\,} $$ where $C$ is a constant depending on the choice of $y_1$ and $y_2$. This identity is useful for verifying linear independence: if $C \neq 0$, then $W \neq 0$ for all $x \in I$, and $y_1, y_2$ are linearly independent. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided) > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting to write the ODE in standard form** ($y'' + Py' + Qy = 0$) before forming the auxiliary equation. The coefficient of $y''$ must be **1**. If it is not 1, divide the entire equation by it first. *(−2 marks if missed.)* > 2. **Confusing the rule for repeated roots** between the constant-coefficient case (multiplier is $x$) and the Cauchy–Euler case (multiplier is $\ln x$). *(−2 to −3 marks if swapped.)* > 3. **Not applying initial conditions explicitly** — students often stop at the general solution, losing 2–3 marks that were meant for the particular constants. > 4. **Forgetting the absolute value** when applying $\ln$ in the reduction-of-order integral; write $\ln \vert w \vert$. > 5. In the **complex-root case**, failing to identify $\alpha = -p/2$ and $\beta = \sqrt{4q - p^{2}}/2$ correctly. The sign convention: complex roots are $\alpha \pm i\beta$, **not** $\alpha \pm \beta i$ (the latter is technically equivalent, but the standard form uses $i\beta$). --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Constant-Coefficient Problem** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]** > > **(a)** Solve the differential equation $\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} - 5\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} + 6\,y = 0$. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Solve the initial value problem $y'' + 4y' + 4y = 0$, $y(0) = 1$, $y'(0) = 0$. **(7 marks)** **Solution to A(a):** The ODE is already in standard form. The auxiliary (characteristic) equation is: $$ m^{2} - 5m + 6 = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad (m-2)(m-3) = 0 $$ So $m_1 = 2$, $m_2 = 3$ (real, distinct). General solution: $$ \boxed{\,y(x) = c_1 e^{2x} + c_2 e^{3x}\,} $$ **Solution to A(b):** Auxiliary equation: $m^{2} + 4m + 4 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; (m+2)^{2} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; m = -2$ (repeated). General solution: $$ y(x) = (c_1 + c_2 x)\,e^{-2x} $$ Apply $y(0) = 1$: $\quad (c_1 + 0)(1) = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; c_1 = 1$. Compute $y'$: $$ y'(x) = c_2\,e^{-2x} + (c_1 + c_2 x)(-2)e^{-2x} = e^{-2x}\!\left[c_2 - 2c_1 - 2 c_2 x\right] $$ Apply $y'(0) = 0$: $\quad c_2 - 2c_1 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; c_2 = 2 c_1 = 2$. Final solution: $$ \boxed{\,y(x) = (1 + 2x)\,e^{-2x}\,} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Cauchy–Euler and Reduction of Order** > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]** > > **(a)** Solve the Cauchy–Euler equation $x^{2}\,\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} - x\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} + y = 0$. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Given that $y_1 = e^{x}$ is a solution of $y'' - y' = 0$, use the **method of reduction of order** to find the general solution. **(7 marks)** **Solution to B(a):** Substitute $x = e^{t}$, so $t = \ln x$. The transformed equation (using Section 3.3) is: $$ [D(D-1) - D + 1]\,y = 0 \quad\text{where } D \equiv \frac{d}{dt} $$ Auxiliary equation: $$ m(m-1) - m + 1 = m^{2} - 2m + 1 = (m-1)^{2} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad m = 1 \text{ (repeated)} $$ In $t$: $y = (c_1 + c_2 t)\,e^{t}$. Reverting to $x$ using $t = \ln x$ and $e^{t} = x$: $$ \boxed{\,y(x) = (c_1 + c_2 \ln x)\,x\,} $$ **Solution to B(b):** Standard form: $y'' - y' = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; y'' + (-1)y' + 0 \cdot y = 0$, so $P(x) = -1$, $Q(x) = 0$. Using the reduction of order formula: $$ y_2 = y_1 \int \frac{e^{-\int P(x)\,dx}}{y_1^{2}}\,dx = e^{x} \int \frac{e^{-\int (-1)\,dx}}{e^{2x}}\,dx = e^{x} \int \frac{e^{x}}{e^{2x}}\,dx = e^{x} \int e^{-x}\,dx $$ $$ y_2 = e^{x}\left(-e^{-x}\right) = -1 $$ The constant $-1$ is a trivial rescaling, so we take $y_2 = 1$ as the second independent solution. General solution: $$ \boxed{\,y(x) = c_1 e^{x} + c_2\,} $$ *Cross-check via characteristic equation:* $m^{2} - m = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; m(m-1) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; m_1 = 0,\; m_2 = 1$, giving $y = c_1 e^{0\cdot x} + c_2 e^{x} = c_1 + c_2 e^{x}$. ✔ --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam** * **Standard form is non-negotiable** — always write the ODE as $y'' + P(x)y' + Q(x)y = 0$ before doing anything else. * **General solution has exactly two arbitrary constants** (matches the order). Never settle for a "solution" with fewer. * **Three cases of the characteristic equation** depend on the **discriminant** $D = p^2 - 4q$: * $D > 0$ → real distinct → $c_1 e^{m_1 x} + c_2 e^{m_2 x}$ * $D = 0$ → real equal → $(c_1 + c_2 x)\,e^{mx}$ * $D < 0$ → complex $\alpha \pm i\beta$ → $e^{\alpha x}(c_1 \cos\beta x + c_2 \sin\beta x)$ * **Abel's Identity** is $W = C e^{-\int P\,dx}$ — it is the fastest way to prove linear independence without messy determinants. * **Reduction of Order formula:** $y_2 = y_1 \displaystyle\int \frac{e^{-\int P(x)\,dx}}{y_1^{2}}\,dx$ — set $P(x)$ as the coefficient of $y'$ **after** standardising. * **Cauchy–Euler** always needs the substitution $x = e^{t}$ — the auxiliary equation is $a m(m-1) + b m + c = 0$, **not** $a m^{2} + bm + c = 0$. * For **Cauchy–Euler** with repeated root, the second solution is $x^{m} \ln x$ (not $x \cdot x^{m}$). * **Wronskian = 0** ⟹ linearly dependent; **Wronskian ≠ 0** ⟹ linearly independent. * **Existence–Uniqueness** requires $P(x)$ and $Q(x)$ continuous on the interval — this is what guarantees a unique solution matching two initial conditions. * **Engineering link:** RLC circuits, spring-mass-damper systems, second-order control systems all reduce to exactly these equations; the cases of $D$ correspond to *overdamped, critically damped, underdamped* responses. > **End of Module 2 Notes — Homogeneous Linear ODEs of Second Order**

topics.title: Superposition Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GYMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE AND PHYSICAL SCIENCE - 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Superposition Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Superposition Principle** is a fundamental property of linear mathematical systems (linear equations, linear differential equations, and linear transformations) which states that the net response produced by two or more stimuli at a given point in space and time is equal to the algebraic sum of the individual responses that would have been produced by each stimulus acting independently.

Mathematically, if $y_1(x)$ is the response of a system to an input $f_1(x)$, and $y_2(x)$ is the response to an input $f_2(x)$, then the response to the combined input $f_1(x) + f_2(x)$ is given by:

$$y(x) = y_1(x) + y_2(x)$$

This principle is the **defining characteristic of linearity** and is built upon two constituent properties:
1. **Additivity:** $T(x_1 + x_2) = T(x_1) + T(x_2)$
2. **Homogeneity (Scaling):** $T(\alpha x) = \alpha T(x)$

When combined, these yield the generalized superposition condition:

$$T(\alpha x_1 + \beta x_2) = \alpha T(x_1) + \beta T(x_2)$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Vector Forces" Picture:**
> Imagine pushing a shopping cart with two friends, Ravi applying a force $F_1$ from the left and Sneha applying a force $F_2$ from the right. If the cart responds linearly (no friction-free wheel assumption at low speeds), the resulting motion (displacement) is exactly the sum of the motions each friend would have caused alone. This is **superposition in action**.

Another classic example is **water waves**: when two ripples cross a pond, the height of the water at any point equals the sum of the heights of each ripple individually. The waves pass through each other and emerge unchanged — a hallmark of linear systems.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold:**

- The principle holds exactly in systems governed by **linear operators** (e.g., resistor networks, harmonic oscillators without damping, quantum mechanical states before measurement).
- It **fails** in nonlinear systems such as those exhibiting **saturation**, **clipping**, or **chaotic behavior** — and in such cases, engineers resort to **numerical solvers** like the Newton-Raphson method.

## 1.3 Why This Matters in GYMAT101

In KTU's 2024 scheme, the Superposition Principle is the **conceptual bridge** between:
- **Linear Algebra** (matrix-vector systems $A\vec{x} = \vec{b}$)
- **Linear Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs)** (homogeneous and non-homogeneous)

Mastery of this principle is mandatory for later modules on **Eigenvalues**, **Fourier Series**, and **Laplace Transforms**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Superposition

### Pillar 1: Additivity
For a linear operator $L$, the sum of two inputs produces the sum of their individual outputs:

$$L(x_1 + x_2) = L(x_1) + L(x_2)$$

### Pillar 2: Homogeneity (Scalar Multiplication)
Scaling the input by a constant scales the output by the same constant:

$$L(\alpha x) = \alpha L(x), \quad \forall \alpha \in \mathbb{R}$$

The **combined superposition** test requires **both** properties to hold simultaneously for all valid inputs.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Principle

- **Step 1:** Verify that the governing equation is **linear** (no products of the unknown function or its derivatives, no nonlinear functions like $\sin(y)$ or $y^2$).
- **Step 2:** Identify the **forcing function** (input) and the **system response** (output).
- **Step 3:** Decompose a complex forcing function into a sum of simpler functions (e.g., using **Fourier decomposition** or **partial fractions**).
- **Step 4:** Solve the system for **each simpler input separately**, obtaining $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n$.
- **Step 5:** **Sum** all individual responses: $y_{total} = y_1 + y_2 + \dots + y_n$.
- **Step 6:** Verify boundary conditions or initial conditions on the final composite solution.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table for Superposition Principle**

| **Concept** | **Mathematical Statement** | **Domain of Validity** | **Key Insight** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Linearity Test | $L(\alpha x_1 + \beta x_2) = \alpha L(x_1) + \beta L(x_2)$ | All linear systems | Combines additivity + homogeneity |
| Superposition (ODE) | If $y_1, y_2$ solve $Ly = f_1, f_2$, then $y_1 + y_2$ solves $Ly = f_1 + f_2$ | Linear ODEs only | Reduces complex problems to simple ones |
| Homogeneous Solution | $y_h = c_1 y_1 + c_2 y_2$ (linear combination of independent solutions) | $n^{th}$ order linear homogeneous ODE | Forms the **complementary function** |
| Particular Solution Sum | $y_p = y_{p_1} + y_{p_2}$ (sum of particular solutions) | Linear non-homogeneous ODEs | Core technique for **method of undetermined coefficients** |
| Matrix Superposition | $A(\vec{x}_1 + \vec{x}_2) = A\vec{x}_1 + A\vec{x}_2$ | Linear transformations | Foundation of matrix algebra |
| Wronskian Test | $W(y_1, y_2) \neq 0 \Rightarrow$ linearly independent solutions | 2nd order linear ODEs | Used to validate superposition basis |
| Convolution Identity | $y(t) = \int_0^t h(t-\tau) f(\tau) d\tau$ | Linear time-invariant (LTI) systems | Generalized superposition for continuous inputs |
| Initial Value Decomposition | $y(x) = y_{tr}(x) + y_{ss}(x)$ | Linear ODEs with inputs | Transient + steady-state response |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Field** | **Application of Superposition Principle** |
|:---|:---|
| **Electrical Circuits** | Analysis of linear circuits with multiple independent sources (Thevenin's and Norton's theorems are direct corollaries). |
| **Structural Engineering** | Computing stresses/deflections in beams under multiple loads. |
| **Quantum Mechanics** | A quantum state is a linear combination (superposition) of basis states; measurement collapses the wave function. |
| **Signal Processing** | Fourier and Laplace transforms decompose signals into superposable basis functions (sines, complex exponentials). |
| **Control Systems** | State-space representation $\dot{\vec{x}} = A\vec{x} + B\vec{u}$ relies on linearity for stability analysis. |
| **Acoustics** | Linear sound waves superpose; nonlinear effects (shock waves) break the principle. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For the KTU 2024 scheme, students must be able to (i) **state** the superposition principle precisely, (ii) **test** whether a given differential equation is linear, and (iii) **apply** the principle to construct general solutions of linear ODEs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Rigorous Derivation: Superposition for Linear Differential Equations

**Theorem (Superposition for Linear ODEs):**
Let $L$ be a linear differential operator of order $n$ defined as:

$$L = a_n(x)\frac{d^n}{dx^n} + a_{n-1}(x)\frac{d^{n-1}}{dx^{n-1}} + \dots + a_1(x)\frac{d}{dx} + a_0(x)$$

If $y_1(x)$ and $y_2(x)$ are solutions to the **homogeneous** equation $L[y] = 0$, then any linear combination $y = c_1 y_1 + c_2 y_2$ (where $c_1, c_2 \in \mathbb{R}$) is also a solution.

**Proof — Step-by-Step:**

Since $y_1$ and $y_2$ are solutions of the homogeneous equation, by definition:

$$
L[y_1] = a_n \frac{d^n y_1}{dx^n} + a_{n-1} \frac{d^{n-1} y_1}{dx^{n-1}} + \dots + a_0 y_1 = 0
$$

$$
L[y_2] = a_n \frac{d^n y_2}{dx^n} + a_{n-1} \frac{d^{n-1} y_2}{dx^{n-1}} + \dots + a_0 y_2 = 0
$$

Now consider the linear combination $y = c_1 y_1 + c_2 y_2$. Apply the operator $L$:

$$
L[y] = L[c_1 y_1 + c_2 y_2]
$$

Using the **linearity of differentiation** (derivatives are linear operators) and the **linearity of the operator $L$**:

$$
L[c_1 y_1 + c_2 y_2] = c_1 L[y_1] + c_2 L[y_2]
$$

Substituting the homogeneous solutions:

$$
L[c_1 y_1 + c_2 y_2] = c_1 \cdot 0 + c_2 \cdot 0 = 0
$$

Therefore, $y = c_1 y_1 + c_2 y_2$ satisfies $L[y] = 0$. $\blacksquare$

> **Conversion Logic:** The key insight is that the operator $L$ is built from derivatives (linear operations) and multiplications by functions $a_i(x)$ (also linear). So $L$ is itself linear, which makes superposition applicable.

## 3.2 Extension: Superposition for Non-Homogeneous Equations

**Theorem:**
If $y_{p_1}$ is a particular solution of $L[y] = f_1(x)$ and $y_{p_2}$ is a particular solution of $L[y] = f_2(x)$, then $y_p = y_{p_1} + y_{p_2}$ is a particular solution of $L[y] = f_1(x) + f_2(x)$.

**Proof:**

Apply $L$ to the sum:

$$
L[y_p] = L[y_{p_1} + y_{p_2}]
$$

By linearity of $L$:

$$
L[y_p] = L[y_{p_1}] + L[y_{p_2}] = f_1(x) + f_2(x)
$$

Hence $y_p = y_{p_1} + y_{p_2}$ solves the combined equation. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Verifying Linearity

**Problem:** Test whether the operator $L[y] = y'' + 3y' - 4y$ is linear.

**Solution:**

Let $y = \alpha y_1 + \beta y_2$ where $\alpha, \beta$ are scalars. Then:

$$
L[\alpha y_1 + \beta y_2] = \frac{d^2}{dx^2}(\alpha y_1 + \beta y_2) + 3 \frac{d}{dx}(\alpha y_1 + \beta y_2) - 4(\alpha y_1 + \beta y_2)
$$

Using the linearity of differentiation:

$$
L[\alpha y_1 + \beta y_2] = \alpha \frac{d^2 y_1}{dx^2} + \beta \frac{d^2 y_2}{dx^2} + 3\alpha \frac{dy_1}{dx} + 3\beta \frac{dy_2}{dx} - 4\alpha y_1 - 4\beta y_2
$$

Grouping the $\alpha$ and $\beta$ terms:

$$
L[\alpha y_1 + \beta y_2] = \alpha \left( y_1'' + 3y_1' - 4y_1 \right) + \beta \left( y_2'' + 3y_2' - 4y_2 \right)
$$

$$
L[\alpha y_1 + \beta y_2] = \alpha L[y_1] + \beta L[y_2]
$$

**Conclusion:** $L$ is linear. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Worked Example: Constructing Solutions via Superposition

**Problem:** Given that $y_1 = e^{2x}$ and $y_2 = e^{-3x}$ are solutions of $y'' + y' - 6y = 0$, find the general solution.

**Solution:**

By the superposition principle, the general solution is the linear combination:

$$
y_h(x) = c_1 e^{2x} + c_2 e^{-3x}
$$

**Verification:** Differentiating:

$$
y_h' = 2c_1 e^{2x} - 3c_2 e^{-3x}
$$

$$
y_h'' = 4c_1 e^{2x} + 9c_2 e^{-3x}
$$

Substituting into the ODE:

$$
(4c_1 e^{2x} + 9c_2 e^{-3x}) + (2c_1 e^{2x} - 3c_2 e^{-3x}) - 6(c_1 e^{2x} + c_2 e^{-3x})
$$

Grouping terms:

$$
= c_1 e^{2x}(4 + 2 - 6) + c_2 e^{-3x}(9 - 3 - 6)
$$

$$
= c_1 e^{2x}(0) + c_2 e^{-3x}(0) = 0
$$

Hence $y_h(x)$ satisfies the ODE. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Worked Example: Non-Homogeneous Equation with Multiple Inputs

**Problem:** Solve $y'' - 4y = e^{x} + 6e^{3x}$ using superposition.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Decompose the input:**
The forcing function $f(x) = e^x + 6e^{3x}$ is naturally split as $f_1(x) = e^x$ and $f_2(x) = 6e^{3x}$.

**Step 2 — Solve $y'' - 4y = e^x$ (call its particular solution $y_{p_1}$):**

Try $y_{p_1} = A e^x$. Then $y_{p_1}'' = A e^x$. Substituting:

$$
A e^x - 4A e^x = e^x \implies -3A = 1 \implies A = -\frac{1}{3}
$$

So $y_{p_1} = -\frac{1}{3} e^x$.

**Step 3 — Solve $y'' - 4y = 6e^{3x}$ (call its particular solution $y_{p_2}$):**

Try $y_{p_2} = B e^{3x}$. Then $y_{p_2}'' = 9B e^{3x}$. Substituting:

$$
9B e^{3x} - 4B e^{3x} = 6e^{3x} \implies 5B = 6 \implies B = \frac{6}{5}
$$

So $y_{p_2} = \frac{6}{5} e^{3x}$.

**Step 4 — Homogeneous solution of $y'' - 4y = 0$:**

Characteristic equation: $m^2 - 4 = 0 \implies m = \pm 2$. So:

$$
y_h = c_1 e^{2x} + c_2 e^{-2x}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply superposition for the particular solution:**

$$
y_p = y_{p_1} + y_{p_2} = -\frac{1}{3} e^x + \frac{6}{5} e^{3x}
$$

**Step 6 — General solution:**

$$
y(x) = c_1 e^{2x} + c_2 e^{-2x} - \frac{1}{3} e^x + \frac{6}{5} e^{3x}
$$

$\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart: Application of Superposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Linear System L_y equals f of x] --> B{Is L a Linear Operator?}
    B -- No --> C[STOP: Superposition Does Not Apply]
    B -- Yes --> D[Decompose Input f into Sum of f1, f2, ..., fn]
    D --> E1[Solve L_y1 equals f1]
    D --> E2[Solve L_y2 equals f2]
    D --> E3[Solve L_yn equals fn]
    E1 --> F[Collect Individual Responses y1, y2, ..., yn]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Apply Superposition: y equals y1 plus y2 plus ... plus yn]
    G --> H[Add Homogeneous Solution y_h if Needed]
    H --> I[Apply Initial or Boundary Conditions]
    I --> J[Final General Solution]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Linear System Superposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1[Input f1 of x] --> sys1[Linear System L]
    input2[Input f2 of x] --> sys1
    inputN[Input fn of x] --> sys1
    sys1 --> dec[Decompose Response]
    dec --> resp1[Response y1]
    dec --> resp2[Response y2]
    dec --> respN[Response yn]
    resp1 --> sum[Summation Block: plus operator]
    resp2 --> sum
    respN --> sum
    sum --> output[Total Response y_total]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Linearity Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Given Operator L and Test Functions y1, y2] --> test1[Test Additivity: L of y1 plus y2 equals L of y1 plus L of y2]
    test1 --> result1{Result True?}
    result1 -- No --> notlinear[Operator is NOT Linear]
    result1 -- Yes --> test2[Test Homogeneity: L of alpha y1 equals alpha L of y1]
    test2 --> result2{Result True?}
    result2 -- No --> notlinear
    result2 -- Yes --> linear[Operator IS Linear: Superposition Valid]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State the Superposition Principle as applied to linear differential equations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Superposition Principle states that if $y_1(x)$ and $y_2(x)$ are two solutions of a linear homogeneous differential equation $L[y] = 0$, then any linear combination $y(x) = c_1 y_1(x) + c_2 y_2(x)$ (where $c_1, c_2$ are arbitrary constants) is also a solution of the same equation. Furthermore, if $y_{p_1}$ and $y_{p_2}$ are particular solutions of $L[y] = f_1(x)$ and $L[y] = f_2(x)$ respectively, then $y_p = y_{p_1} + y_{p_2}$ is a particular solution of $L[y] = f_1(x) + f_2(x)$. The principle is a direct consequence of the linearity of the operator $L$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What are the two essential conditions that a differential equation must satisfy for the Superposition Principle to be applicable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The two essential conditions are:
> 1. **Additivity:** The operator $L$ must satisfy $L[y_1 + y_2] = L[y_1] + L[y_2]$ for all admissible functions $y_1, y_2$. **(1.5 marks)**
> 2. **Homogeneity:** The operator $L$ must satisfy $L[\alpha y] = \alpha L[y]$ for all scalars $\alpha$ and all admissible functions $y$. **(1.5 marks)**
>
> Both conditions together constitute **linearity**. If either condition fails, the equation is nonlinear, and the Superposition Principle cannot be applied.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** Verify that $y_1 = e^{x}$ and $y_2 = xe^{x}$ are two linearly independent solutions of the differential equation $y'' - 2y' + y = 0$. Hence state the general solution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Superposition Principle, find the general solution of the differential equation $y'' - 2y' + y = 2e^{x} + 3x$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1:** Compute derivatives of $y_1 = e^x$:
$y_1' = e^x$ and $y_1'' = e^x$.

**Step 2:** Substitute into the ODE:
$y_1'' - 2y_1' + y_1 = e^x - 2e^x + e^x = 0$ ✓ **(2 marks)**

**Step 3:** Compute derivatives of $y_2 = xe^x$:
Using the product rule:
$y_2' = e^x + xe^x = (1+x)e^x$
$y_2'' = e^x + (1+x)e^x = (2+x)e^x$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the ODE:
$y_2'' - 2y_2' + y_2 = (2+x)e^x - 2(1+x)e^x + xe^x$
$= e^x[(2+x) - 2(1+x) + x]$
$= e^x[2 + x - 2 - 2x + x]$
$= e^x[0] = 0$ ✓ **(2 marks)**

**Step 5:** Check linear independence using the Wronskian:

$$
W(y_1, y_2) = \begin{vmatrix} e^x & xe^x \\ e^x & (1+x)e^x \end{vmatrix} = e^x \cdot (1+x)e^x - xe^x \cdot e^x = e^{2x}[(1+x) - x] = e^{2x} \neq 0
$$

Since $W \neq 0$, the solutions are linearly independent. **(2 marks)**

**Step 6:** State the general solution by superposition:

$$
y_h(x) = c_1 e^x + c_2 x e^x
$$

**(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1 — Decompose the input:**
$f(x) = 2e^x + 3x = f_1(x) + f_2(x)$ where $f_1(x) = 2e^x$ and $f_2(x) = 3x$.

**Step 2 — Solve $y'' - 2y' + y = 2e^x$ for $y_{p_1}$:**
Since $e^x$ is already a solution of the homogeneous equation, try $y_{p_1} = Ax^2 e^x$.

Differentiating:
$y_{p_1}' = 2Ax e^x + Ax^2 e^x = A(2x + x^2)e^x$
$y_{p_1}'' = A(2 + 4x + x^2)e^x$

Substituting into the ODE:
$A(2 + 4x + x^2)e^x - 2A(2x + x^2)e^x + Ax^2 e^x = 2e^x$

Simplifying the bracket:
$A[(2 + 4x + x^2) - 2(2x + x^2) + x^2] = 2$
$A[2 + 4x + x^2 - 4x - 2x^2 + x^2] = 2$
$A[2] = 2 \implies A = 1$

So $y_{p_1} = x^2 e^x$. **(3 marks)**

**Step 3 — Solve $y'' - 2y' + y = 3x$ for $y_{p_2}$:**
Try a polynomial: $y_{p_2} = ax + b$.

Then $y_{p_2}' = a$ and $y_{p_2}'' = 0$.

Substituting:
$0 - 2a + ax + b = 3x$
$(a)x + (-2a + b) = 3x + 0$

Matching coefficients:
- Coefficient of $x$: $a = 3$
- Constant: $-2(3) + b = 0 \implies b = 6$

So $y_{p_2} = 3x + 6$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Apply Superposition for the total particular solution:**

$$
y_p = y_{p_1} + y_{p_2} = x^2 e^x + 3x + 6
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — General solution:**

$$
y(x) = c_1 e^x + c_2 x e^x + x^2 e^x + 3x + 6
$$

**(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Show that the differential operator $L = x^2 \dfrac{d^2}{dx^2} - x\dfrac{d}{dx} + 1$ is linear. Test the validity of the superposition principle on $L[y] = 0$ using $y_1 = x$ and $y_2 = x \ln(x)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using superposition, find the particular integral of $\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} - 4\dfrac{dy}{dx} + 4y = e^{2x} + \sin(3x)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1 — Show linearity:**
Let $y = \alpha y_1 + \beta y_2$. Then:

$$
L[\alpha y_1 + \beta y_2] = x^2 \frac{d^2}{dx^2}(\alpha y_1 + \beta y_2) - x\frac{d}{dx}(\alpha y_1 + \beta y_2) + (\alpha y_1 + \beta y_2)
$$

$$
= \alpha \left( x^2 y_1'' - x y_1' + y_1 \right) + \beta \left( x^2 y_2'' - x y_2' + y_2 \right)
$$

$$
= \alpha L[y_1] + \beta L[y_2]
$$

Hence $L$ is linear. **(3 marks)**

**Step 2 — Verify $y_1 = x$ is a solution:**
$y_1' = 1, y_1'' = 0$. Then $L[y_1] = x^2(0) - x(1) + x = 0$ ✓ **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Verify $y_2 = x \ln(x)$ is a solution:**
$y_2 = x \ln(x)$, $y_2' = \ln(x) + 1$, $y_2'' = \dfrac{1}{x}$.

$L[y_2] = x^2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{x} - x(\ln(x) + 1) + x \ln(x) = x - x \ln(x) - x + x \ln(x) = 0$ ✓ **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Confirm superposition:**
By linearity, $y = c_1 x + c_2 x \ln(x)$ is also a solution for any constants $c_1, c_2$. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation:**
$m^2 - 4m + 4 = 0 \implies (m-2)^2 = 0 \implies m = 2$ (repeated root).

Homogeneous solution: $y_h = (c_1 + c_2 x) e^{2x}$.

**Step 2 — Solve $y'' - 4y' + 4y = e^{2x}$ for $y_{p_1}$:**
Since $e^{2x}$ and $xe^{2x}$ are both in the homogeneous solution, try $y_{p_1} = Ax^2 e^{2x}$.

$y_{p_1}' = 2Ax e^{2x} + 2Ax^2 e^{2x} = A(2x + 2x^2)e^{2x}$
$y_{p_1}'' = A(2 + 4x + 4x + 4x^2)e^{2x} = A(2 + 8x + 4x^2)e^{2x}$

Substituting:
$A(2 + 8x + 4x^2)e^{2x} - 4A(2x + 2x^2)e^{2x} + 4Ax^2 e^{2x} = e^{2x}$

Coefficient of $e^{2x}$:
$A[2 + 8x + 4x^2 - 8x - 8x^2 + 4x^2] = 1$
$A[2] = 1 \implies A = \frac{1}{2}$

So $y_{p_1} = \dfrac{1}{2} x^2 e^{2x}$. **(3 marks)**

**Step 3 — Solve $y'' - 4y' + 4y = \sin(3x)$ for $y_{p_2}$:**
Try $y_{p_2} = A \cos(3x) + B \sin(3x)$.

$y_{p_2}' = -3A \sin(3x) + 3B \cos(3x)$
$y_{p_2}'' = -9A \cos(3x) - 9B \sin(3x)$

Substituting:
$(-9A \cos 3x - 9B \sin 3x) - 4(-3A \sin 3x + 3B \cos 3x) + 4(A \cos 3x + B \sin 3x) = \sin 3x$

Grouping $\cos(3x)$ and $\sin(3x)$ terms:
- $\cos(3x)$: $-9A - 12B + 4A = -5A - 12B = 0$
- $\sin(3x)$: $-9B + 12A + 4B = 12A - 5B = 1$

From the first equation: $A = -\dfrac{12B}{5}$. Substituting into the second:
$12 \left(-\dfrac{12B}{5}\right) - 5B = 1 \implies -\dfrac{144B}{5} - 5B = 1 \implies -\dfrac{169B}{5} = 1 \implies B = -\dfrac{5}{169}$

Then $A = -\dfrac{12}{5} \cdot \left(-\dfrac{5}{169}\right) = \dfrac{12}{169}$.

So $y_{p_2} = \dfrac{12}{169}\cos(3x) - \dfrac{5}{169}\sin(3x)$. **(3 marks)**

**Step 4 — Apply Superposition:**

$$
y_p = y_{p_1} + y_{p_2} = \frac{1}{2} x^2 e^{2x} + \frac{12}{169}\cos(3x) - \frac{5}{169}\sin(3x)
$$

**(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Assuming superposition works for nonlinear equations:** Students often apply superposition to equations like $y'' + y^2 = 0$ or $y' = \sin(y)$. The examiner will award **zero marks** if you use superposition on a nonlinear ODE. Always verify linearity first.
> 2. **Forgetting the homogeneous part:** When asked for the "general solution," students sometimes write only the particular integral. Always include $y_h = c_1 y_1 + c_2 y_2$ (with arbitrary constants).
> 3. **Resonance blind spot:** When the forcing function matches a homogeneous solution (as in $e^{2x}$ in Question B part b), you must multiply by $x^k$ until the trial is linearly independent. Missing this loses 3 marks instantly.
> 4. **Wronskian sign errors:** When checking linear independence, sign errors in the determinant are common. Write out the matrix explicitly and compute the $2 \times 2$ determinant step by step.
> 5. **Skipping the linearity proof:** If asked to "verify linearity," you must explicitly show $L[\alpha y_1 + \beta y_2] = \alpha L[y_1] + \beta L[y_2]$. A vague statement like "the equation is linear" earns no marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Superposition Principle**

- **Definition:** For a linear operator $L$, $L(\alpha y_1 + \beta y_2) = \alpha L(y_1) + \beta L(y_2)$. **(Core)**
- **Two conditions:** Additivity + Homogeneity = Linearity. **(Test before applying)**
- **Homogeneous ODE:** $y_h = c_1 y_1 + c_2 y_2 + \dots + c_n y_n$ (linear combination of $n$ independent solutions). **(Fundamental result)**
- **Non-homogeneous ODE:** $y_p = \sum y_{p_i}$ where each $y_{p_i}$ solves $L[y] = f_i(x)$. **(Superposition of particular solutions)**
- **General solution formula:** $y = y_h + y_p$ (homogeneous + particular). **(Memorize)**
- **Wronskian test:** $W(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n) \neq 0 \Rightarrow$ linear independence. **(Validation tool)**
- **Validity:** Only for **linear** systems. **Nonlinear** = no superposition. **(Boundary of applicability)**
- **Resonance rule:** If trial solution matches homogeneous, multiply by $x$ (or $x^k$) until distinct. **(Special case handling)**
- **Engineering relevance:** Circuit analysis, structural mechanics, quantum mechanics, control systems, signal processing. **(Application awareness)**
- **Common errors:** Applying to nonlinear equations, omitting $y_h$, missing resonance, sign errors in Wronskian. **(Examiner traps)**
- **KTU exam weightage:** Typically 3–7 marks in Part A and 7–14 marks in Part B of ESE Module 2. **(High-yield topic)**
- **Related KTU topics to study next:** Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors, Method of Undetermined Coefficients, Variation of Parameters, Fourier Series, Laplace Transforms. **(Sequencing)**
- **Memory aid:** "**L**inear **A**dditivity + **H**omogeneity" → **LAH** → **Superposition is valid**. **(Mnemonic)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Solution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GYMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE AND PHYSICAL SCIENCE - 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Solution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

Consider the **second-order linear ordinary differential equation (ODE)** with constant coefficients in its most general non-homogeneous form:

$$a \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + b \frac{dy}{dx} + c\,y = f(x), \quad a, b, c \in \mathbb{R}, \; a \neq 0$$

The **General Solution** of this equation is the complete family of all possible solutions, defined as:

$$y(x) = y_{c}(x) + y_{p}(x)$$

where:
- $y_{c}(x)$ is the **Complementary Function (CF)** — the general solution of the associated *homogeneous* equation $a y'' + b y' + c y = 0$.
- $y_{p}(x)$ is the **Particular Integral (PI)** — any *one* specific solution of the full non-homogeneous equation $a y'' + b y' + c y = f(x)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** The General Solution always contains exactly **two arbitrary constants** (for a 2nd-order ODE), because the order of the differential equation determines the number of independent arbitrary constants in the general solution (per the existence-uniqueness theorem).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a custom house (the **General Solution**):
- The **frame and structure** of the house — walls, roof shape, number of floors — is fixed. This is the **Complementary Function (CF)**. It is determined solely by the *intrinsic physics* of the system (the equation itself, ignoring the external forcing).
- The **interior decoration and furniture** — paint, sofa, curtains — is your personal choice. This is the **Particular Integral (PI)**. It depends on the *external force* or *input* applied ($f(x)$).
- The "two arbitrary constants" $C_1, C_2$ are like the **dimensions of the room** (length and width) that you can change freely without breaking the structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** General Solution = Structure (CF) + Specific Response (PI). The CF captures *natural behavior*; the PI captures *forced behavior*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The equation is **linear** in $y$ and its derivatives.
- $a, b, c$ are **real constants** (coefficients).
- $f(x)$ is the **forcing function** (input / non-homogeneous term).
- Examples of $f(x)$ encountered in KTU module problems: polynomials, exponentials $e^{kx}$, sines/cosines $\sin(kx), \cos(kx)$, and products thereof.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the General Solution

The General Solution $y = y_c + y_p$ has two logically independent parts. They are computed by **separate procedures** because they describe fundamentally different mathematical phenomena:

**Part A — Complementary Function (CF):**
1. Set the right-hand side to zero: $f(x) = 0$.
2. Substitute trial solution $y = e^{mx}$ into the homogeneous equation.
3. This yields the **Auxiliary (Characteristic) Equation**: $a m^{2} + b m + c = 0$.
4. Solve for roots $m_1, m_2$.
5. Write CF based on the **nature of the roots** (real, repeated, complex).

**Part B — Particular Integral (PI):**
1. Choose a method based on the form of $f(x)$:
   - **Method of Undetermined Coefficients** — when $f(x)$ is a polynomial, exponential, trig, or simple product.
   - **Method of Variation of Parameters** — universal, works for *any* $f(x)$.
2. Substitute, equate coefficients, and solve for unknowns.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why substitute $y = e^{mx}$?** Because exponentials preserve their form under differentiation ($d/dx(e^{mx}) = m e^{mx}$). This converts a *differential* equation into an *algebraic* equation in $m$.
- **Why two arbitrary constants?** A second-order ODE requires two initial/boundary conditions to pin down a unique solution; the two constants are placeholders for that information.
- **Why does CF represent natural response?** In circuits and mechanical systems, the CF corresponds to the *transient* response that decays (or grows) due to the system's internal dynamics.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Component** | **Case / Form of Roots** | **Formula** |
|---|---|---|
| Auxiliary Equation | General 2nd-order linear, constant coefficients | $a m^{2} + b m + c = 0$ |
| CF — Real & Distinct | $m_1 \neq m_2$ (real) | $y_{c} = C_{1} e^{m_{1} x} + C_{2} e^{m_{2} x}$ |
| CF — Real & Repeated | $m_1 = m_2 = m$ | $y_{c} = (C_{1} + C_{2} x) e^{m x}$ |
| CF — Complex Conjugate | $m = \alpha \pm i \beta$ | $y_{c} = e^{\alpha x}\!\left( C_{1} \cos \beta x + C_{2} \sin \beta x \right)$ |
| PI — General Operator Form | $y_{p} = \dfrac{1}{f(D)}\, F(x)$ | $y_{p} = \dfrac{1}{aD^{2}+bD+c}\, f(x)$ |
| PI — Polynomial RHS | $f(x)$ is a polynomial in $x$ | Substitute $y_{p} = A_{0} + A_{1} x + A_{2} x^{2} + \cdots$ |
| PI — Exponential RHS | $f(x) = k\, e^{nx}$ | Try $y_{p} = A e^{nx}$ (unless $n$ is a root of aux. eqn) |
| PI — Trigonometric RHS | $f(x) = a \cos nx + b \sin nx$ | Try $y_{p} = A \cos nx + B \sin nx$ |
| Wronskian (Variation of Parameters) | For $y_1, y_2$ independent solutions | $W = \begin{vmatrix} y_{1} & y_{2} \\ y_{1}' & y_{2}' \end{vmatrix}$ |
| Variation of Parameters — $u_1$ | First parameter | $u_{1}(x) = -\int \dfrac{y_{2}(x)\, f(x)}{a\, W}\, dx$ |
| Variation of Parameters — $u_2$ | Second parameter | $u_{2}(x) = \int \dfrac{y_{1}(x)\, f(x)}{a\, W}\, dx$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder:** When $f(D)$ contains a root of the auxiliary equation as a factor, the trial PI must be **multiplied by $x$** (or $x^{k}$) to avoid duplication with the CF. This is the most common KTU 2024 trap!

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Field** | **Application of General Solution** |
|---|---|
| **Electrical Circuits** | RLC circuit analysis: $L \frac{d^{2}i}{dt^{2}} + R \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{C} i = V(t)$ — CF = transient, PI = steady-state |
| **Mechanical Vibrations** | Damped oscillator: $m \ddot{x} + c \dot{x} + k x = F(t)$ — over/under/critically damped cases |
| **Control Systems** | Stability analysis: roots of aux. equation determine if system is stable (negative real parts) |
| **Signal Processing** | Convolution with Green's function; CF relates to impulse response |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Complementary Function (Three Cases)

#### Case 1: Real and Distinct Roots

We assume the homogeneous equation $a y'' + b y' + c y = 0$ admits a solution of the form $y = e^{mx}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
y &= e^{mx} \\
y' &= m e^{mx} \\
y'' &= m^{2} e^{mx}
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute into the homogeneous equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
a (m^{2} e^{mx}) + b (m e^{mx}) + c (e^{mx}) &= 0 \\
e^{mx} (a m^{2} + b m + c) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Since $e^{mx} \neq 0$ for all real $x$, we must have:

$$a m^{2} + b m + c = 0$$

This is the **Auxiliary Equation** (a quadratic in $m$). If its roots are real and distinct $m_1 \neq m_2$, then $e^{m_1 x}$ and $e^{m_2 x}$ are linearly independent solutions. The CF is:

$$y_{c} = C_{1} e^{m_{1} x} + C_{2} e^{m_{2} x}$$

#### Case 2: Real and Repeated Roots

When the discriminant of the auxiliary equation is zero, $b^{2} - 4ac = 0$, we get a single repeated root $m = m_1 = m_2 = -b/(2a)$. Only one exponential solution is obtained: $y_1 = e^{m_1 x}$. To find a second independent solution, we use **reduction of order**: try $y_2 = v(x) e^{m_1 x}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
y_2' &= v' e^{m_1 x} + m_1 v e^{m_1 x} \\
y_2'' &= v'' e^{m_1 x} + 2 m_1 v' e^{m_1 x} + m_1^{2} v e^{m_1 x}
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute into the homogeneous ODE and simplify (the $v e^{m_1 x}$ terms cancel by definition of the root):

$$e^{m_1 x} (a v'' + (2 a m_1 + b) v') = 0$$

Since $2 a m_1 + b = 0$ (from the root formula), this reduces to $a v'' = 0$, so $v'' = 0$. Integrating twice:

$$v = C x + D \implies y_2 = x e^{m_1 x}$$

Thus the CF is:

$$y_{c} = (C_{1} + C_{2} x)\, e^{m_1 x}$$

#### Case 3: Complex Conjugate Roots

If the discriminant is negative, the auxiliary equation has complex roots $m = \alpha \pm i \beta$ where:

$$\alpha = -\frac{b}{2a}, \quad \beta = \frac{\sqrt{\vert 4ac - b^{2} \vert}}{2a}$$

The general solution of the homogeneous equation is:

$$y_{c} = e^{\alpha x}\!\left( C_{1} \cos \beta x + C_{2} \sin \beta x \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This form is derived by applying **Euler's formula** $e^{i \beta x} = \cos \beta x + i \sin \beta x$ to the complex exponentials and recombining to eliminate imaginary parts.

### 3.2 Method of Undetermined Coefficients — Full Procedure

**Step 1: Identify the form of $f(x)$.**

**Step 2: Propose a trial $y_p$ based on the following table:**

| **Form of $f(x)$** | **Trial Form of $y_p$** | **Modification if $m$ is a root of Aux. Eqn** |
|---|---|---|
| Polynomial of degree $n$ | $A_0 + A_1 x + \cdots + A_n x^{n}$ | Multiply by $x$ (or higher power) |
| $k e^{n x}$ | $A e^{n x}$ | Multiply by $x$ (or $x^{k}$) |
| $a \cos nx + b \sin nx$ | $A \cos nx + B \sin nx$ | Multiply by $x$ |

**Step 3: Substitute $y_p$ into the full ODE, expand, and equate coefficients of like terms on both sides.**

**Step 4: Solve the resulting linear system for the unknown coefficients.**

### 3.3 Worked Example: Method of Undetermined Coefficients

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Style Problem — Full Model Solution**

**Problem:** Find the General Solution of $y'' - 3 y' + 2 y = 6 e^{3x}$.

**Step 1 — Auxiliary Equation:**

$$m^{2} - 3 m + 2 = 0 \implies (m-1)(m-2) = 0 \implies m_1 = 1,\; m_2 = 2$$

Both roots are real and distinct, so:

$$y_{c} = C_{1} e^{x} + C_{2} e^{2x}$$

**Step 2 — Trial PI:** The forcing function is $6 e^{3x}$, so we try $y_{p} = A e^{3x}$. Note that $3$ is **not** a root of the auxiliary equation, so no $x$-multiplication is needed.

**Step 3 — Differentiate and substitute:**

$$\begin{aligned}
y_p &= A e^{3x} \\
y_p' &= 3 A e^{3x} \\
y_p'' &= 9 A e^{3x}
\end{aligned}$$

Plug into the ODE:

$$9 A e^{3x} - 3(3 A e^{3x}) + 2(A e^{3x}) = 6 e^{3x}$$

$$(9A - 9A + 2A) e^{3x} = 6 e^{3x} \implies 2A = 6 \implies A = 3$$

**Step 4 — General Solution:**

$$\boxed{y = C_{1} e^{x} + C_{2} e^{2x} + 3 e^{3x}}$$

### 3.4 Worked Example: Resonant (Failure) Case

**Problem:** Find the General Solution of $y'' - 5 y' + 6 y = 4 e^{2x}$.

**Step 1 — Auxiliary Equation:**

$$m^{2} - 5 m + 6 = 0 \implies (m-2)(m-3) = 0 \implies m_1 = 2,\; m_2 = 3$$

**Step 2 — Trial PI (Modified):** The RHS is $4 e^{2x}$ and $2$ **is** a root of the auxiliary equation. Therefore the naive trial $A e^{2x}$ would duplicate part of the CF and lead to failure. We must multiply by $x$:

$$y_{p} = A x e^{2x}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate using the product rule:**

$$\begin{aligned}
y_p &= A x e^{2x} \\
y_p' &= A e^{2x} + 2 A x e^{2x} \\
y_p'' &= 2 A e^{2x} + 2 A e^{2x} + 4 A x e^{2x} = 4 A e^{2x} + 4 A x e^{2x}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the ODE:**

$$(4 A e^{2x} + 4 A x e^{2x}) - 5(A e^{2x} + 2 A x e^{2x}) + 6(A x e^{2x}) = 4 e^{2x}$$

Group like terms ($x e^{2x}$ and $e^{2x}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
x e^{2x} \text{ coefficient:} &\quad 4A - 10A + 6A = 0 \quad \text{(always 0, as expected)} \\
e^{2x} \text{ coefficient:} &\quad 4A - 5A = -A
\end{aligned}$$

So $-A = 4 \implies A = -4$.

**Step 5 — General Solution:**

$$\boxed{y = C_{1} e^{2x} + C_{2} e^{3x} - 4 x e^{2x}}$$

### 3.5 Method of Variation of Parameters — Full Derivation

This is the **universal method** for finding PI, valid for *any* $f(x)$.

**Setup:** Let $y_1(x)$ and $y_2(x)$ be two linearly independent solutions of the homogeneous equation. Assume the PI has the form:

$$y_p = u_1(x) y_1(x) + u_2(x) y_2(x)$$

**Constraint 1 (eliminate second derivatives of $u$'s):**

$$u_1' y_1 + u_2' y_2 = 0$$

Differentiating $y_p$ once:

$$y_p' = u_1 y_1' + u_2 y_2'$$

Differentiating again:

$$y_p'' = u_1 y_1'' + u_2 y_2'' + u_1' y_1' + u_2' y_2'$$

**Substitute into $a y_p'' + b y_p' + c y_p = f(x)$:**

$$a(u_1' y_1' + u_2' y_2') + (a u_1 y_1'' + b u_1 y_1' + c u_1 y_1) + (a u_2 y_2'' + b u_2 y_2' + c u_2 y_2) = f(x)$$

The terms in parentheses vanish because $y_1, y_2$ satisfy the homogeneous ODE. We get:

**Constraint 2:**

$$a(u_1' y_1' + u_2' y_2') = f(x) \implies u_1' y_1' + u_2' y_2' = \frac{f(x)}{a}$$

**Solving the Linear System:** Combine both constraints to solve for $u_1'$ and $u_2'$:

$$\begin{aligned}
u_1' y_1 + u_2' y_2 &= 0 \\
u_1' y_1' + u_2' y_2' &= \frac{f(x)}{a}
\end{aligned}$$

Using Cramer's rule with the Wronskian $W = y_1 y_2' - y_2 y_1'$:

$$u_1' = \frac{-y_2 \cdot f(x)/a}{W} = -\frac{y_2 f(x)}{a W}, \quad u_2' = \frac{y_1 \cdot f(x)/a}{W} = \frac{y_1 f(x)}{a W}$$

**Integrate** to obtain $u_1$ and $u_2$:

$$u_1(x) = -\int \frac{y_2(x)\, f(x)}{a\, W(x)}\, dx, \quad u_2(x) = \int \frac{y_1(x)\, f(x)}{a\, W(x)}\, dx$$

The particular integral is then:

$$y_p = u_1 y_1 + u_2 y_2$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Flow: General Solution Procedure

The following Mermaid block maps the full algorithm for obtaining the General Solution of a 2nd-order linear ODE with constant coefficients. This satisfies the KTU 2024 expectation that students can identify the correct sub-procedure based on the form of $f(x)$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartA[Start: 2nd Order Linear ODE ay'' + by' + cy = f(x)] --> Step1[Step 1: Set f x = 0 to get homogeneous equation]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Form Auxiliary Equation: a m^2 + b m + c = 0]
    Step2 --> DiscCheck{Discriminant b^2 - 4ac ?}
    DiscCheck --> Case1[Greater than zero: Real Distinct Roots m1, m2]
    DiscCheck --> Case2[Equal to zero: Repeated Root m1 = m2]
    DiscCheck --> Case3[Less than zero: Complex Roots alpha plus/minus i beta]
    Case1 --> CFOut[CF = C1 e to m1 x plus C2 e to m2 x]
    Case2 --> CFOut2[CF = C1 plus C2 x times e to m1 x]
    Case3 --> CFOut3[CF = e to alpha x times C1 cos beta x plus C2 sin beta x]
    StartA --> ForceCheck{Form of f x ?}
    ForceCheck --> PolyChoice[f x is Polynomial: Use Undetermined Coefficients]
    ForceCheck --> ExpChoice[f x is Exponential: Use Undetermined Coefficients]
    ForceCheck --> TrigChoice[f x is Trigonometric: Use Undetermined Coefficients]
    ForceCheck --> GenChoice[f x is General: Use Variation of Parameters]
    PolyChoice --> RootCheck{n equals root of Aux Eqn ?}
    ExpChoice --> RootCheck
    TrigChoice --> RootCheck
    RootCheck --> YesPath[Yes: Multiply trial PI by x to power k]
    RootCheck --> NoPath[No: Use trial PI as is]
    YesPath --> SubSolve[Solve for unknown coefficients]
    NoPath --> SubSolve
    GenChoice --> VoPSolve[Integrate Wronskian based formulas for u1 and u2]
    SubSolve --> PIOut[Obtain PI]
    VoPSolve --> PIOut
    CFOut --> FinalAdd[Final: y = CF plus PI]
    CFOut2 --> FinalAdd
    CFOut3 --> FinalAdd
    PIOut --> FinalAdd
    FinalAdd --> EndNode[End: General Solution with 2 arbitrary constants]
```

### 4.2 Decision Logic: When to Use Which PI Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: f x] --> B{Is f x polynomial, exp, or trig?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Does trial form clash with CF?}
    B -- No --> D[Use Method of Variation of Parameters]
    C -- Yes --> E[Multiply trial by x to power k]
    C -- No --> F[Substitute trial directly]
    E --> G[Solve for coefficients]
    F --> G
    G --> H[PI obtained]
    D --> H
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: CF + PI in Physical Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[External Input f x] --> ODE[Linear System described by ODE]
    ODE --> Natural[Natural Response equals CF]
    ODE --> Forced[Forced Response equals PI]
    Natural --> Output[Total Output y = CF + PI]
    Forced --> Output
    Output --> Use1[Electrical: Transient plus Steady State]
    Output --> Use2[Mechanical: Free plus Forced Vibration]
    Output --> Use3[Control: Homogeneous plus Particular Solution]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Define the term "General Solution" of a linear differential equation. State its two components and explain their physical significance in a 2nd-order system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **General Solution** of a linear ODE is the complete family of all solutions, containing as many arbitrary constants as the order of the equation. For a 2nd-order linear ODE $a y'' + b y' + c y = f(x)$, it has the form:
$$y = y_c + y_p$$
where $y_c$ is the **Complementary Function** (general solution of the homogeneous equation, representing the system's natural/transient response) and $y_p$ is the **Particular Integral** (any one specific solution of the non-homogeneous equation, representing the forced/steady-state response to $f(x)$). **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]**
*If the auxiliary equation of a 2nd-order linear ODE has roots $m_1, m_2$ where $m_1 = 2$ and $m_2 = -3$, write down the Complementary Function. What happens if both roots are equal?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For real and distinct roots $m_1 = 2, m_2 = -3$, the CF is:
$$y_c = C_1 e^{2x} + C_2 e^{-3x}$$
If both roots are equal to a common value $m$, the CF takes the form:
$$y_c = (C_1 + C_2 x) e^{mx}$$
The factor $x$ is multiplied to obtain a second linearly independent solution (by reduction of order). **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student must attempt exactly one full question from the choice provided.

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Find the Complementary Function of the differential equation:
$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} - 4 \frac{dy}{dx} + 4 y = 0$$

**(b) [7 marks]** Hence, find the General Solution of:
$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} - 4 \frac{dy}{dx} + 4 y = e^{2x} \sin x$$

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Complementary Function (7 marks):**

The auxiliary equation is $m^{2} - 4m + 4 = 0$.

$$\begin{aligned}
m^{2} - 4m + 4 &= 0 \\
(m - 2)^{2} &= 0 \\
m &= 2, \; 2 \quad \text{(repeated real root)}
\end{aligned}$$

Since the roots are real and equal, the CF is:

$$y_c = (C_1 + C_2 x) e^{2x}$$

**(Award: A1 — Forming auxiliary eqn (1 mark), A2 — Solving roots (2 marks), A3 — Applying repeated-root formula (2 marks), A4 — Final CF with two arbitrary constants (2 marks) = 7 marks)**

**Part (b) — General Solution (7 marks):**

Since $m = 2$ is a root of the auxiliary equation, the trial $y_p = A e^{2x} \sin x$ requires checking for duplication. The forcing term is $e^{2x} \sin x$; the corresponding complex exponent $2 \pm i$ is **not** a root of the auxiliary equation (root is $2$ only). Hence the trial $y_p$ is valid as stated.

Let $y_p = e^{2x} (A \cos x + B \sin x)$.

**Compute derivatives:**

$$\begin{aligned}
y_p &= e^{2x}(A \cos x + B \sin x) \\
y_p' &= 2 e^{2x}(A \cos x + B \sin x) + e^{2x}(-A \sin x + B \cos x) \\
    &= e^{2x}\!\left[ (2A + B) \cos x + (2B - A) \sin x \right] \\
y_p'' &= 2 e^{2x}\!\left[ (2A + B) \cos x + (2B - A) \sin x \right] \\
      &\quad + e^{2x}\!\left[ -(2A + B) \sin x + (2B - A) \cos x \right] \\
    &= e^{2x}\!\left[ (4A + 2B + 2B - A) \cos x + (4B - 2A - 2A - B) \sin x \right] \\
    &= e^{2x}\!\left[ (3A + 4B) \cos x + (3B - 4A) \sin x \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Substitute into the ODE:**

$$y_p'' - 4 y_p' + 4 y_p = e^{2x} \sin x$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\begin{aligned}
& e^{2x}\!\left[ (3A + 4B) \cos x + (3B - 4A) \sin x \right] \\
- &\; 4 e^{2x}\!\left[ (2A + B) \cos x + (2B - A) \sin x \right] \\
+ &\; 4 e^{2x}(A \cos x + B \sin x) = e^{2x} \sin x
\end{aligned}$$

**Collect $\cos x$ and $\sin x$ terms separately:**

Coefficient of $\cos x$:
$$(3A + 4B) - 4(2A + B) + 4A = 3A + 4B - 8A - 4B + 4A = -A$$

Coefficient of $\sin x$:
$$(3B - 4A) - 4(2B - A) + 4B = 3B - 4A - 8B + 4A + 4B = -B$$

So the equation reduces to:

$$-A \cos x - B \sin x = \sin x$$

Equating coefficients: $-A = 0 \implies A = 0$, and $-B = 1 \implies B = -1$.

Thus $y_p = -e^{2x} \sin x$.

**General Solution:**

$$\boxed{y = (C_1 + C_2 x) e^{2x} - e^{2x} \sin x}$$

**(Award: A1 — Valid trial PI (1 mark), A2 — Differentiation of $y_p$ (2 marks), A3 — Substitution and equating coefficients (2 marks), A4 — Solving for $A, B$ and final GS (2 marks) = 7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Find the General Solution of:
$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + 2 \frac{dy}{dx} + 5 y = \sin 2x$$

**(b) [7 marks]** Solve using the Method of Variation of Parameters:
$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + y = \tan x, \quad 0 < x < \frac{\pi}{2}$$

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — General Solution by Undetermined Coefficients (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Auxiliary Equation**

$$m^{2} + 2m + 5 = 0 \implies m = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 - 20}}{2} = \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{-16}}{2} = -1 \pm 2i$$

Complex conjugate roots with $\alpha = -1, \beta = 2$.

**Step 2: Complementary Function**

$$y_c = e^{-x}(C_1 \cos 2x + C_2 \sin 2x)$$

**Step 3: Trial PI**

The forcing is $\sin 2x$. Note that $\pm 2i$ are *not* roots of the auxiliary equation, so the trial:

$$y_p = A \cos 2x + B \sin 2x$$

is valid (no $x$-multiplication needed).

**Step 4: Differentiate and substitute**

$$\begin{aligned}
y_p' &= -2A \sin 2x + 2B \cos 2x \\
y_p'' &= -4A \cos 2x - 4B \sin 2x
\end{aligned}$$

Plug into the ODE:

$$(-4A \cos 2x - 4B \sin 2x) + 2(-2A \sin 2x + 2B \cos 2x) + 5(A \cos 2x + B \sin 2x) = \sin 2x$$

Collect terms:

Coefficient of $\cos 2x$: $-4A + 4B + 5A = A + 4B = 0$
Coefficient of $\sin 2x$: $-4B - 4A + 5B = B - 4A = 1$

From the first: $A = -4B$. Substituting: $B - 4(-4B) = 1 \implies 17B = 1 \implies B = 1/17$, and $A = -4/17$.

**Step 5: General Solution**

$$\boxed{y = e^{-x}(C_1 \cos 2x + C_2 \sin 2x) - \frac{4}{17} \cos 2x + \frac{1}{17} \sin 2x}$$

**(Award: 7 marks distributed across root-finding (2), CF (1), trial PI (1), substitution & algebra (2), final GS (1))**

---

**Part (b) — Variation of Parameters (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Homogeneous Solutions**

The auxiliary equation $m^{2} + 1 = 0$ has roots $m = \pm i$. So $y_1 = \cos x$, $y_2 = \sin x$.

**Step 2: Wronskian**

$$W = \begin{vmatrix} \cos x & \sin x \\ -\sin x & \cos x \end{vmatrix} = \cos^{2} x + \sin^{2} x = 1$$

**Step 3: Compute $u_1$ and $u_2$** (with $a = 1$)

$$\begin{aligned}
u_1(x) &= -\int \frac{y_2 \tan x}{W}\, dx = -\int \frac{\sin x \cdot \tan x}{1}\, dx = -\int \frac{\sin^{2} x}{\cos x}\, dx \\
       &= -\int \frac{1 - \cos^{2} x}{\cos x}\, dx = -\int (\sec x - \cos x)\, dx \\
       &= -\ln \vert \sec x + \tan x \vert + \sin x
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
u_2(x) &= \int \frac{y_1 \tan x}{W}\, dx = \int \cos x \cdot \tan x\, dx = \int \sin x\, dx = -\cos x
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Construct $y_p$**

$$\begin{aligned}
y_p &= u_1 y_1 + u_2 y_2 \\
    &= \left( -\ln \vert \sec x + \tan x \vert + \sin x \right) \cos x + (-\cos x) \sin x \\
    &= -\cos x \ln \vert \sec x + \tan x \vert + \sin x \cos x - \sin x \cos x \\
    &= -\cos x \ln \vert \sec x + \tan x \vert
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: General Solution**

$$\boxed{y = C_1 \cos x + C_2 \sin x - \cos x \ln \vert \sec x + \tan x \vert}$$

**(Award: 7 marks distributed across identifying $y_1, y_2$ (1), Wronskian (1), $u_1$ integration (2), $u_2$ integration (1), final GS (2))**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $x$-multiplication rule (–2 to –3 marks):** When the trial $y_p$ duplicates a term already present in CF (e.g., $f(x) = e^{mx}$ and $m$ is a root of the auxiliary equation), students often get $A = 0$ or an indeterminate $0/0$ result. Always check: does any part of your trial PI appear in the CF? If yes, multiply by $x$ until no duplication remains.
>
> 2. **Wrong formula for repeated-root CF (–2 marks):** Many students incorrectly write $y_c = C_1 e^{mx} + C_2 e^{mx}$ for repeated roots. The correct form is $y_c = (C_1 + C_2 x) e^{mx}$.
>
> 3. **Confusing CF roots with PI trial (–2 marks):** For $f(x) = e^{nx}$, the value $n$ in the trial $A e^{nx}$ is checked against the *auxiliary equation roots*, NOT against the constant coefficients $a, b, c$ directly.
>
> 4. **Dropping the arbitrary constants in the final answer (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU strictly requires the General Solution to contain **exactly two independent arbitrary constants** for a 2nd-order ODE. Forgetting $C_1$ or $C_2$ is a common slip.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to combine CF and PI (–1 mark):** Writing only the CF or only the PI is incomplete. The General Solution is always $y = y_c + y_p$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **General Solution** of a 2nd-order linear ODE with constant coefficients = **Complementary Function (CF) + Particular Integral (PI)**.
- The **CF** comes from solving the auxiliary equation $a m^{2} + b m + c = 0$. Its form depends on the **nature of the roots**:
  - Real & distinct $\Rightarrow$ $C_1 e^{m_1 x} + C_2 e^{m_2 x}$
  - Real & repeated $\Rightarrow$ $(C_1 + C_2 x) e^{m x}$
  - Complex $\alpha \pm i \beta \Rightarrow$ $e^{\alpha x}(C_1 \cos \beta x + C_2 \sin \beta x)$
- The **PI** is found by:
  - **Undetermined Coefficients** (fast, but only for specific $f(x)$).
  - **Variation of Parameters** (slow, but universal).
- **Critical Rule:** If the trial PI clashes with CF, **multiply by $x$** (or $x^{k}$) until no clash.
- The **General Solution** must always contain **two linearly independent arbitrary constants**.
- In **engineering applications** (RLC circuits, mechanical vibrations), CF = transient, PI = steady-state.
- **Wronskian** $W = y_1 y_2' - y_2 y_1'$ must **never be zero** (otherwise $y_1, y_2$ are dependent, and variation of parameters fails).
- Always **substitute back** at the end to verify the solution satisfies the original ODE — this is the KTU examiner's quick check.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Homogeneous Linear ODEs of Second Order with Constant Coefficients (Method to Find General Solution, Solution of Linear Initial Value Problem)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GYMAT101 - MATHEMATICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE AND PHYSICAL SCIENCE - 1
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Homogeneous Linear ODEs of Second Order with Constant Coefficients (Method to Find General Solution, Solution of Linear Initial Value Problem)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Second Order Linear Ordinary Differential Equation with Constant Coefficients** is an equation of the form:

$$\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} + P\frac{dy}{dx} + Qy = R(x)$$

where **P** and **Q** are real constants, and **R(x)** is a function of the independent variable *x* (taken as 0 here for the homogeneous case).

The equation is called:

- **Linear**: The dependent variable *y* and all its derivatives appear in the first degree (no products like $y \cdot y'$ or non-linear functions like $\sin(y)$).
- **Homogeneous**: The right-hand side is identically zero, i.e., $R(x) = 0$.
- **With Constant Coefficients**: The coefficients of $y''$, $y'$, and $y$ are constants, not functions of $x$.

The general **standard form** of a homogeneous second order linear ODE with constant coefficients is:

$$ay'' + by' + cy = 0$$

where $a, b, c \in \mathbb{R}$ and $a \neq 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A 2nd order ODE requires **two** linearly independent solutions to construct the general solution, because the solution space of a 2nd order linear ODE is a **two-dimensional vector space**. Hence we always need **two arbitrary constants** $c_1$ and $c_2$ in the general solution.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the equation $ay'' + by' + cy = 0$ as describing a **simple harmonic oscillator** — for example, a mass attached to a spring floating in a viscous fluid.

- The term $ay''$ represents the **inertia** of the system (acceleration of the mass).
- The term $by'$ represents the **damping** force (friction proportional to velocity).
- The term $cy$ represents the **restoring force** of the spring (proportional to displacement).

The equation asks the question: *"What motion of the mass naturally satisfies all three forces simultaneously?"*

The **characteristic equation** $ar^2 + br + c = 0$ is the algebraic fingerprint of this physical system:

- If the roots are **real and distinct** → the system is **overdamped** (returns to rest without oscillation, like a shock absorber).
- If the roots are **real and equal** → the system is **critically damped** (fastest return to rest without oscillation).
- If the roots are **complex conjugates** → the system is **underdamped** (oscillates with decreasing amplitude, like a guitar string after being plucked).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Wronskian Connection:** Two solutions $y_1$ and $y_2$ of a 2nd order linear homogeneous ODE are linearly independent if and only if their Wronskian $W(y_1, y_2) \neq 0$. The three cases we study arise because the form of the fundamental solutions depends on the nature of the characteristic roots.

## 1.3 The Initial Value Problem (IVP)

A **Linear Initial Value Problem** consists of the differential equation together with prescribed values of the solution and its derivative at a starting point:

$$ay'' + by' + cy = 0, \quad y(x_0) = y_0, \quad y'(x_0) = y_0'$$

The **Existence and Uniqueness Theorem** (guaranteed for this case since coefficients are constants) ensures exactly one solution exists. The arbitrary constants $c_1$ and $c_2$ are determined by solving a $2 \times 2$ linear system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Auxiliary (Characteristic) Equation

We postulate an exponential trial solution of the form $y = e^{rx}$ where $r$ is a constant. Substituting into $ay'' + by' + cy = 0$:

$$a(r^2 e^{rx}) + b(re^{rx}) + c(e^{rx}) = 0$$

Factoring out $e^{rx}$ (which is never zero):

$$e^{rx}(ar^2 + br + c) = 0$$

Since $e^{rx} \neq 0$ for all real *x*, the equation reduces to the **Auxiliary Equation (A.E.)** or **Characteristic Equation**:

$$\boxed{ar^2 + br + c = 0}$$

This is a quadratic in *r* with discriminant $\Delta = b^2 - 4ac$.

## 2.2 The Three Cases — Theory of Solution

### **Case 1: Real and Distinct Roots** ($\Delta > 0$)

Let $r_1$ and $r_2$ be the two distinct real roots ($r_1 \neq r_2$). Then $e^{r_1 x}$ and $e^{r_2 x}$ are linearly independent solutions. The general solution is:

$$y(x) = c_1 e^{r_1 x} + c_2 e^{r_2 x}$$

**Why this works:** Since $r_1, r_2$ are distinct, the ratio $e^{r_1 x}/e^{r_2 x} = e^{(r_1 - r_2)x}$ is non-constant, confirming linear independence.

### **Case 2: Real and Equal (Repeated) Roots** ($\Delta = 0$)

When $b^2 = 4ac$, the auxiliary equation has only one root $r = -\dfrac{b}{2a}$ of multiplicity 2. Only one exponential solution $e^{rx}$ is obtained directly. The second linearly independent solution is found via **reduction of order** to be $x e^{rx}$. The general solution is:

$$y(x) = (c_1 + c_2 x)e^{rx}$$

**Intuition:** Repeated roots signal that the system is at the "boundary" between oscillation and decay — the solution has just enough freedom to decay without oscillating, but needs an extra $x$ factor to remain independent.

### **Case 3: Complex Conjugate Roots** ($\Delta < 0$)

The roots are $r = \alpha \pm i\beta$ where $\alpha = -\dfrac{b}{2a}$ and $\beta = \dfrac{\sqrt{4ac - b^2}}{2a}$. Substituting into Euler's formula and using linear independence of sine and cosine, the general solution is:

$$y(x) = e^{\alpha x}\left(c_1 \cos(\beta x) + c_2 \sin(\beta x)\right)$$

**Physical meaning:** $\alpha$ controls the decay (or growth, if $\alpha > 0$) of the oscillation envelope, and $\beta$ is the angular frequency of oscillation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Case | Discriminant | Roots $r_1, r_2$ | General Solution $y(x)$ | Physical Interpretation |
|:----:|:------------:|:----------------:|:------------------------|:------------------------|
| **1** | $\Delta = b^2 - 4ac > 0$ | Real and distinct $r_1 \neq r_2$ | $c_1 e^{r_1 x} + c_2 e^{r_2 x}$ | Overdamped system |
| **2** | $\Delta = b^2 - 4ac = 0$ | Real and equal $r_1 = r_2 = r$ | $(c_1 + c_2 x) e^{r x}$ | Critically damped system |
| **3** | $\Delta = b^2 - 4ac < 0$ | Complex conjugates $\alpha \pm i\beta$ | $e^{\alpha x}(c_1 \cos \beta x + c_2 \sin \beta x)$ | Underdamped system |

### Key Supplementary Formulas

| Quantity | Formula |
|:---------|:--------|
| Characteristic equation | $ar^2 + br + c = 0$ |
| Sum of roots | $r_1 + r_2 = -b/a$ |
| Product of roots | $r_1 \cdot r_2 = c/a$ |
| Real part of complex root | $\alpha = -b/(2a)$ |
| Imaginary part of complex root | $\beta = \sqrt{4ac - b^2}/(2a)$ |
| IVP system (for $y(x_0) = y_0, y'(x_0) = y_0'$) | Two linear equations in $c_1, c_2$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This ODE family is foundational in:
